title: Automated Validator Report Reader
description: Given a simulated validator report listing six findings (three warnings, three errors) against a 15-node sample graph, the learner can correctly classify each finding's severity, identify the specific node or edge it affects on the rendered graph, and correctly determine whether the graph is safe to publish given the findings present.
creator: Learning Graphs
author: Dan McCreary
subject: Learning Graphs

educational.topic: Automated Validation, Finding Severity, Publish Readiness
learningObjectives: Locate each validator finding's affected node or edge on the rendered graph
Classify findings by severity and determine which ones block publication
Explain why three reported errors can produce only two blockers
Justify a publish/no-publish decision by naming the specific unresolved errors

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Click-to-locate report triage with a publish-readiness judgment check
keyQuestions: Three errors are reported. How many actually block publication, and why?
Why does the report still list DUP-003 if it has already been fixed?
BAL-002 flags a category at 33%. Against only 15 concepts, is that worth acting on?
Which finding would you fix first, and what makes it more urgent than the others?
commonMisconceptions: That every error blocks publication — DUP-003 is an error and is already resolved
That warnings can be ignored — they are judgment calls that still need a reader
That severity alone decides publish readiness, without reading what each finding says
That a threshold like 30% means the same thing on a 15-concept graph as on a 200-concept one
assessmentOpportunities: Ask learners to name each finding's affected element before reading its explanation
Ask the class to count blockers after sorting by severity, then surface the resolved error
Ask learners to justify their publish decision by naming specific finding IDs
